children.name: Plethodon
children.description: The largest genus of salamanders. Breathe entirely through moist skin and the mouth lining — no lungs at all. Eggs laid in terrestrial sites hatch as miniature adults, bypassing the aquatic larval stage. Among the most abundant vertebrates in Appalachian deciduous forest.

children.name: Plethodon glutinosus
children.description: Secretes a sticky mucus that glues the jaws of predators together; among the most abundant vertebrates in Appalachian forest.

children.name: Plethodon cinereus
children.description: One of the most numerous vertebrates in northeastern North American forests; estimated at up to 3 per square metre in optimal habitat; no aquatic stage.

children.name: Plethodon jordani
children.description: A red-cheeked woodland salamander endemic to the Great Smoky Mountains, a model species in studies of competition and mimicry among Appalachian Plethodon.

children.name: Plethodon yonahlossee
children.description: A large, handsome woodland salamander with a chestnut-red back, restricted to the southern Blue Ridge of the Appalachians.

children.name: Plethodon serratus
children.description: A small woodland salamander of the Ouachita and Ozark highlands and scattered Appalachian populations, closely resembling P. cinereus but genetically distinct. It inhabits moist deciduous forest and rocky terrain, sheltering under cover objects during dry periods.

children.name: Plethodon vehiculum
children.description: The Pacific Northwest counterpart of the red-backed salamander, common under logs and bark in moist Douglas-fir and red alder forests from British Columbia to northern California. Highly philopatric individuals maintain year-round home ranges beneath individual cover objects.

children.name: Plethodon vandykei
children.description: A slender, striped woodland salamander of the Olympics and Cascades in Washington state, associated with moist talus slopes, seeps, and streamside spray zones. It is often found alongside the equally restricted Cope's giant salamander and is listed as a species of concern in Washington.

children.name: Plethodon kentucki
children.description: A dark, dorsally black woodland salamander endemic to the Cumberland Plateau of Kentucky and adjacent states, where it inhabits mesic forest with limestone outcrops. It is ecologically similar to P. glutinosus but restricted to a narrower elevational band.

children.name: Plethodon dorsalis
children.description: A small but vividly patterned salamander named for the sinuous zigzag stripe running along its back, found on limestone-rich hillsides of the Interior Highlands and southern Indiana. It breeds terrestrially and is active at the surface only during mild, wet weather.

children.name: Plethodon wehrlei
children.description: A large, dark Appalachian woodland salamander with scattered white or brassy spots, occupying forested rocky slopes from New York to North Carolina. It is notable for using crevices, caves, and mines as hibernacula and for maintaining stable populations despite forest clearance nearby.

children.name: Desmognathus
children.description: Stream-side salamanders.

children.name: Desmognathus ochrophaeus
children.description: Semi-aquatic; common along Appalachian streams; lays terrestrial egg clutches.

children.name: Desmognathus fuscus
children.description: A stout, stream-side salamander with powerful jaws and a pale line from eye to jaw angle, abundant along brooks in eastern North America.

children.name: Desmognathus quadramaculatus
children.description: One of the largest lungless salamanders, a powerful predator of fast-flowing Appalachian streams with a jet-black belly.

children.name: Desmognathus monticola
children.description: A handsome, mottled stream salamander of Appalachian seeps and rocky brooks, named for its sleek seal-like appearance.

children.name: Desmognathus wrighti
children.description: The smallest desmognathine, barely 4 cm total length, living at high elevations in the southern Blue Ridge where it forages on the forest floor among mosses. It lacks an aquatic larval stage entirely, with miniature adults hatching directly from terrestrial eggs.

children.name: Desmognathus conanti
children.description: A boldly spotted dusky salamander of the Gulf Coastal Plain and lower Mississippi Valley, inhabiting seeps, springs, and small streams. It was long treated as a subspecies of D. fuscus but is now recognized as a distinct species based on morphological and molecular data.

children.name: Desmognathus aeneus
children.description: A tiny, slender desmognathine of forested seeps in the southern Appalachians and Cumberland Plateau, with a well-defined dorsal stripe and pale sides. Its small body size and preference for saturated moss mats and seepage areas distinguish it from sympatric congeners.

children.name: Desmognathus marmoratus
children.description: A large, highly aquatic species of swift, boulder-strewn Appalachian rivers, with a flattened head and shovel-like snout used to probe beneath stones for aquatic invertebrates. It maintains external gills through its extended larval period and is considered a conservation indicator for clean headwater streams.

children.name: Eurycea
children.description: Cave and stream salamanders.

children.name: Eurycea lucifuga
children.description: Vivid orange with black spots; inhabits cave entrances and twilight zones of Appalachian limestone caves.

children.name: Eurycea rathbuni
children.description: Translucent skin, vestigial eyes, bright red external gills; lives entirely in the Edwards Plateau aquifer; critically endangered.

children.name: Eurycea bislineata
children.description: A slender yellowish brook salamander with two dark dorsal stripes, common along streams and seeps across northeastern North America.

children.name: Eurycea wilderae
children.description: A southern Appalachian two-lined salamander of mountain streams, distinguished by genetics and a more elaborate courtship than its northern relatives.

children.name: Eurycea nana
children.description: A tiny, fully aquatic, neotenic salamander restricted to the spring-fed headwaters of the San Marcos River in Texas; federally listed as Threatened.

children.name: Eurycea sosorum
children.description: A gilled, neotenic salamander found only in Barton Springs, Austin, Texas; Endangered and highly sensitive to groundwater quality.

children.name: Eurycea multiplicata
children.description: A paedomorphic salamander of cold Ozark and Ouachita spring runs, retaining external gills and a larval body form throughout life. Its common name refers to the visible rib impressions along its compressed flanks, and it occupies a narrow range in Arkansas and Oklahoma.

children.name: Eurycea tynerensis
children.description: A permanently aquatic, gilled salamander endemic to gravel-bed streams and springs of the Ozark Plateau in northeastern Oklahoma and adjacent Arkansas. It is one of the most range-restricted vertebrates in the central US and is listed as Threatened.

children.name: Eurycea pterophila
children.description: A slender, neotenic cave salamander known only from a single spring system—Fern Bank Springs—in Hays County, Texas, making it one of the most geographically restricted vertebrates in North America. It inhabits the aquatic hyporheic zone and is sensitive to groundwater extraction.

children.name: Eurycea tridentifera
children.description: A highly cave-adapted salamander of the Edwards Aquifer in Comal County, Texas, with vestigial eyes and pale, translucent skin. Its three-pronged premaxillary teeth, reflected in the scientific name, help distinguish it from the sympatric Texas Blind Salamander.

children.name: Eurycea latitans
children.description: A slender, eyeless cave salamander restricted to the Cascade Caverns cave system of Kendall County, Texas, within the Edwards Plateau. It is fully neotenic and depends entirely on clean groundwater, making it vulnerable to regional aquifer drawdown and surface contamination.

children.name: Gyrinophilus
children.description: The spring salamanders — large, salmon-pink stream predators of Appalachian springs and caves.

children.name: Gyrinophilus porphyriticus
children.description: A large, pinkish-orange salamander of cold Appalachian springs and headwater streams, an active nocturnal predator of other salamanders.

children.name: Gyrinophilus palleucus
children.description: A pale, permanently gilled cave salamander of limestone cave streams in the southeastern US; listed as Threatened and restricted to a few cave systems.

children.name: Gyrinophilus subterraneus
children.description: A cave-dwelling spring salamander known from only a single cave system in Greenbrier County, West Virginia, making it one of the rarest vertebrates in the eastern United States. Permanently neotenic, it retains large bushy gills and has reduced pigmentation compared to surface-dwelling Gyrinophilus.

children.name: Pseudotriton
children.description: The red and mud salamanders — stout, brightly colored stream salamanders of eastern North America.

children.name: Pseudotriton ruber
children.description: A vivid red, black-spotted salamander of springs and seepages whose color may mimic the toxic red eft stage of the eastern newt.

children.name: Pseudotriton montanus
children.description: A stocky, brownish-red salamander of muddy springs and boggy seepages in the eastern US, distinguished from the Red Salamander by its blunter snout and less distinct black spots. It occupies wetter, muddier microhabitats than P. ruber and is less frequently encountered at the surface.

children.name: Stereochilus
children.description: The many-lined salamander — a monotypic genus of sluggish, tannin-stained coastal plain streams in the southeastern United States.

children.name: Stereochilus marginatus
children.description: A small, aquatic salamander of slow, darkwater streams and backwaters on the Atlantic Coastal Plain from Virginia to Georgia, often found among dead leaves and root mats at the stream bottom. It is the sole member of its genus and has an extended larval period of up to two years before metamorphosis.

children.name: Aneides
children.description: Arboreal salamanders.

children.name: Aneides lugubris
children.description: Climbs trees; prehensile tail; strong jaw muscles deliver a painful bite; California oaks.

children.name: Aneides aeneus
children.description: A flattened, lichen-patterned climbing salamander that lives in damp rock crevices of the Appalachians; declining and protected across much of its range.

children.name: Aneides flavipunctatus
children.description: A robust, dark climbing salamander of coastal northern California and southern Oregon, often speckled with fine white or gold flecks.

children.name: Aneides hardii
children.description: A small, slender climbing salamander restricted to the high-elevation spruce-fir forests of the Sacramento and Capitan mountains of New Mexico, one of the most isolated plethodontid populations in North America. It lives under bark and in rocky crevices and is listed as Threatened by the state of New Mexico.

children.name: Batrachoseps
children.description: The slender salamanders — worm-like, four-toed lungless salamanders of western North America that burrow and feed using a projectile tongue.

children.name: Batrachoseps attenuatus
children.description: An extremely elongate, worm-like salamander with tiny limbs and four toes, abundant under logs and litter in coastal California woodlands.

children.name: Batrachoseps pacificus
children.description: A slender, burrowing salamander of the Channel Islands and adjacent southern California coast, active during the wet winter months.

children.name: Batrachoseps nigriventris
children.description: A slender salamander of southern California oak woodlands and chaparral, characterized by its distinctly dark or black ventral surface contrasting with a gray-brown dorsum. It is active during cool, wet winters and aestivates deep in the soil during hot, dry summers.

children.name: Batrachoseps major
children.description: The largest of the slender salamanders, widespread in southern California and Baja California from coastal scrub to urban gardens, where irrigated lawns have allowed it to colonize drier areas than most congeners. It is frequently found in flowerpots and compost heaps and is the species most commonly encountered by the public.

children.name: Batrachoseps stebbinsi
children.description: A range-restricted slender salamander of the southern Sierra Nevada foothills and Tehachapi Mountains in Kern County, California, inhabiting rock outcrops and moist talus in blue oak woodland. It is listed as Threatened under the California ESA due to its small total area of occupancy.

children.name: Batrachoseps wrighti
children.description: The most northerly slender salamander, confined to the old-growth and mature Douglas-fir forests of the western Cascade Range in Oregon. It shelters in the bark and decaying wood of large fallen logs and is sensitive to the removal of old-growth forest structure.

children.name: Hydromantes
children.description: Tongue-projecting salamanders.

children.name: Hydromantes platycephalus
children.description: Fires a ballistic tongue longer than its own body to catch prey in under 20 milliseconds; bare granite specialist in the Sierra Nevada.

children.name: Hydromantes italicus
children.description: Sole plethodontid in Europe; Tertiary relic isolated in Italian cave systems; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Hydromantes brunus
children.description: A web-toed salamander confined to limestone and mossy talus along the Merced River canyon in California; range-restricted and of conservation concern.

children.name: Hydromantes shastae
children.description: A critically range-restricted web-toed salamander known only from a few limestone cave systems near Shasta Lake in northern California, making it among the rarest salamanders in the United States. Construction of Shasta Dam flooded a significant portion of its original range, and it remains Federally Threatened.

children.name: Hydromantes samweli
children.description: A recently described web-toed salamander from the Trinity Alps region of northern California, separated from H. platycephalus based on molecular and morphological data. It inhabits granite and marble outcrops at high elevations, sheltering in deep rock fissures during the hot, dry summer season.

children.name: Ensatina
children.description: A single ring-species complex famed in evolutionary biology, encircling California's Central Valley as a chain of intergrading subspecies.

children.name: Ensatina eschscholtzii
children.description: A classic ring species whose color forms grade around the Central Valley but fail to interbreed where the ends meet; constricts its tail base when threatened.

children.name: Speleomantes
children.description: The European cave salamanders — the only plethodontids in the Old World, endemic to Italy, Sardinia and a corner of France.

children.name: Speleomantes strinatii
children.description: A lungless cave salamander of the Maritime Alps in Italy and France, sheltering in caves and damp rock crevices; one of the few plethodontids outside the Americas.

children.name: Speleomantes imperialis
children.description: A robust cave salamander endemic to southeastern Sardinia, restricted to humid limestone caves and gorges; protected under EU law.

children.name: Speleomantes flavus
children.description: A distinctively patterned cave salamander endemic to the central and eastern limestone mountains of Sardinia, recognizable by irregular gold or yellowish dorsal blotches on a dark background. Like all Speleomantes it is a direct developer, laying terrestrial eggs that hatch as miniature adults.

children.name: Speleomantes ambrosii
children.description: A cave and rupestral salamander of the Ligurian Apennines and northwestern Tuscany in Italy, closely associated with limestone outcrops, cave entrances, and talus slopes in dense Mediterranean forest. It can project its entire tongue apparatus to capture prey in a matter of milliseconds.

children.name: Bolitoglossa
children.description: The mushroomtongue salamanders — the most species-rich salamander genus, tropical American climbers with webbed feet and a high-speed projectile tongue.

children.name: Bolitoglossa mexicana
children.description: A boldly patterned arboreal salamander of lowland Mexican and Central American forests, using fully webbed feet to climb wet vegetation.

children.name: Bolitoglossa robusta
children.description: A robust montane salamander of Costa Rica and Panama with a distinctive pale ring near the tail base, inhabiting cloud forest.

children.name: Bolitoglossa rostrata
children.description: A slender, long-snouted climbing salamander of highland Guatemalan and Mexican cloud forests, named for its unusually pointed rostrum. It forages nocturnally in bromeliads and on mossy branches, using its fully webbed, pad-like feet to grip wet surfaces.

children.name: Bolitoglossa subpalmata
children.description: A common cloud-forest climbing salamander of the Costa Rican and western Panamanian highlands, one of the best-studied neotropical plethodontids. It lays terrestrial eggs that are brooded by the female in epiphytic bromeliads, bypassing the aquatic larval stage entirely.

children.name: Bolitoglossa flavimembris
children.description: A boldly colored climbing salamander of the Pacific slopes of Guatemala and Mexico, with strikingly yellow limbs contrasting against a dark dorsum. It inhabits humid montane forest from 1000 to 2500 m elevation and is directly threatened by agricultural conversion of cloud forest.

children.name: Bolitoglossa occidentalis
children.description: A small, dark climbing salamander of humid lowland and foothill forests from southwestern Mexico through Central America, one of the most widespread members of the genus. It is an opportunistic colonist of cacao and coffee plantations, giving it some tolerance for modified habitats.

children.name: Bolitoglossa adspersa
children.description: A widespread cloud-forest salamander of the northern Andes from Venezuela to Ecuador, among the highest-elevation Bolitoglossa species. It climbs mossy shrubs and tree ferns in páramo-edge habitats and is used as an indicator of intact Andean forest in biodiversity assessments.

children.name: Bolitoglossa sombra
children.description: A cryptically colored Andean climbing salamander described from the Colombian and Ecuadorian Andes, where it inhabits the dense understory of montane rainforest above 1500 m. Its dark, shadowy pattern gives it excellent camouflage on bark and leaf litter, consistent with its species name.

children.name: Oedipina
children.description: The worm salamanders — extremely elongate, burrowing plethodontids of lowland and foothill forests in Central America and northwestern South America, with minuscule limbs and up to 20 costal grooves.

children.name: Oedipina complex
children.description: A slender, black worm salamander of humid lowland forests from Honduras to Colombia, among the most morphologically elongate of all salamanders. It burrows through deep leaf litter and soil with tiny vestigial limbs, surfacing only during heavy rains.

children.name: Oedipina gracilis
children.description: A diminutive worm salamander of montane cloud forests in Guatemala and Chiapas, Mexico, with a proportionally smaller head and more slender body than most congeners. Its extreme elongation and four-toed feet are adaptations to life in the narrow spaces between soil particles and root mats.

children.name: Oedipina uniformis
children.description: A uniformly dark, pencil-thin burrowing salamander of lowland Atlantic-slope forests in Costa Rica and Nicaragua, named for its unpatterned, monochrome dorsum. It is most commonly found by turning logs along forest trails and is threatened by deforestation of the Caribbean lowlands.

children.name: Nototriton
children.description: The moss salamanders — small, cryptic plethodontids of Central American cloud forests, typically found in humid moss mats, bromeliads, and dense epiphytic vegetation.

children.name: Nototriton picadoi
children.description: A small, brown moss salamander of the Costa Rican highlands, first described from specimens found inside bromeliads in the Cordillera Central. It is rarely encountered and has been the subject of detailed natural history studies linking plethodontid diversification to epiphyte-rich montane habitats.

children.name: Nototriton abscondens
children.description: A secretive, rarely observed moss salamander of the Talamanca mountain range in Costa Rica and Panama, typically found only when epiphytic moss mats are carefully searched at night. Its Latin name, meaning hidden or concealed, reflects how seldom it is detected despite persistent survey effort.

children.name: Nototriton richardi
children.description: A cloud-forest moss salamander of the highlands of Guatemala and Honduras, associated with dense moss blankets on oaks and humid conifers above 2000 m. It was named in honor of a herpetologist who contributed significantly to understanding Central American plethodontid diversity.

children.name: Chiropterotriton
children.description: The bat-eared salamanders — a Mexican genus of cave and cloud-forest plethodontids with large, often bat-like parotoid glands or expanded head structures, found primarily in the highlands of eastern Mexico.

children.name: Chiropterotriton dimidiatus
children.description: A small, dark plethodontid of the cloud forests and caves of the Sierra Madre Oriental in eastern Mexico, one of the founding species of the genus when it was split from Pseudoeurycea. It inhabits moist rock crevices and is active during and after heavy rains.

children.name: Chiropterotriton chondrostega
children.description: A cave-adapted bat-eared salamander of the limestone karst of Tamaulipas and Nuevo León, Mexico, named for the cartilaginous roof of its mouth visible in dissection. It is among the most cave-associated members of its genus, with reduced eyes and paler coloration compared to surface relatives.

children.name: Chiropterotriton mosaueri
children.description: A highland plethodontid of the pine-oak forests of the eastern Mexican highlands, closely related to C. dimidiatus and similarly restricted to humid montane microhabitats. Its populations are highly fragmented as a result of agricultural expansion into the Sierra Madre cloud forest zone.

children.name: Pseudoeurycea
children.description: The false brook salamanders — a diverse genus of Mexican and Central American highland plethodontids occupying cloud forests, pine-oak woodlands, and cave systems across the Sierra Madre ranges.

children.name: Pseudoeurycea bellii
children.description: One of the largest and most robust members of its genus, with a stout body and vivid orange-and-black patterning, widespread across the highlands of central Mexico above 2000 m. It inhabits pine-oak and cloud forests and was the nominate species when the genus was first described.

children.name: Pseudoeurycea leprosa
children.description: A rough-textured, dark false brook salamander of the transverse volcanic belt of central Mexico, often found beneath logs and rocks in humid montane meadows and forest edges. Its granular skin texture, reflected in its specific epithet, provides camouflage among the lichen-covered stones it favors.

children.name: Pseudoeurycea gadovii
children.description: A slender, brown false brook salamander of the cloud forests of Veracruz and Oaxaca, Mexico, collected in the vicinity of high-altitude coffee farms and secondary forest. It was named in honor of Hans Friedrich Gadow, a German-British zoologist who made foundational contributions to the study of Mexican herpetology.

children.name: Thorius
children.description: The pygmy salamanders — the world's smallest salamanders, endemic to humid montane forests of the Sierra Madre del Sur in Oaxaca and Guerrero, Mexico, with some species fitting on a fingernail.

children.name: Thorius pennatulus
children.description: One of the smallest tetrapods on Earth at under 30 mm total length, from the cloud forests of the Sierra de Juárez in Oaxaca. Its miniaturized body plan, including reduced bone counts and simplified musculature, is a product of extreme paedomorphic evolution in the plethodontid lineage.

children.name: Thorius narisovalis
children.description: A minute Oaxacan pygmy salamander distinguished by its broadly oval nostrils, which separate it from co-occurring Thorius species. It inhabits bromeliads, moss cushions, and decaying wood in cloud forests above 2000 m, foraging on microarthropods using a proportionally large projectile tongue.

children.name: Thorius arboreus
children.description: A scansorial pygmy salamander of the Oaxacan highlands, spending much of its life in the canopy of cloud-forest trees rather than on the ground, an unusual habit among the otherwise terrestrial Thorius. With a total length rarely exceeding 27 mm, it is among the most structurally miniaturized vertebrates known to science.

children.name: Isthmura
children.description: A recently resurrected genus of large, robust plethodontids from the montane forests of Mexico and Guatemala, previously lumped within Pseudoeurycea; distinguished by molecular and osteological characters.

children.name: Isthmura bellii
children.description: One of the largest plethodontids in Mexico, reaching over 18 cm total length, found in humid cloud forests and pine-oak woodlands of the transverse volcanic belt above 2500 m. Long classified as Pseudoeurycea bellii, molecular studies established it as the type species of the resurrected genus Isthmura.

children.name: Isthmura nigra
children.description: A uniformly black, high-altitude plethodontid of the Sierra de los Cuchumatanes of Guatemala and adjacent Chiapas, among the most range-restricted members of its genus. It inhabits cold, mossy cloud forests above 3000 m and is threatened by agricultural encroachment on the high-altitude plateau.
